search_queries_used: official EAACI urticaria guideline 2022 definition classification
candidate_sources_rejected: paywalled detailed urticaria text that could not be opened
anaphylaxis treatment tables as routine urticaria management
rejection_reasons: Patient pages are not clinician guidance.
Unopened detailed text was not used for item-level claims.
Urticaria alone must not be treated as anaphylaxis.
population_applicability: Partial: exact for urticaria definition and anaphylaxis warning recognition; routine acute or chronic urticaria history, testing, and management are not fully covered by opened text.
setting_applicability: International allergy and emergency guidance; UAE referral and emergency pathways require local clinician review.
UAE_applicability: No exact UAE urticaria guideline was identified; international guidance requires UAE clinician review.
recency_verification: The current official EAACI urticaria page and official RCUK dedicated anaphylaxis page were opened on 2026-07-12.
superseded_check: No newer international urticaria guideline was identified on EAACI; the RCUK page still identifies May 2021 as its current dedicated anaphylaxis guideline.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: Supports mapped urticaria, hives, wheals, angioedema, acute or chronic classification, and compatible observed skin or swelling fields.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: Defines urticaria as a mast-cell-driven disease presenting with wheals, angioedema, or both and distinguishes acute and chronic forms.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: Supports mapped acute versus chronic context and quality-of-life impact only; detailed management text was not accessible.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: States that chronic spontaneous or inducible urticaria can impair quality of life and work or school performance and that the guideline covers classification, diagnosis, and management.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: Supports mapped breathing, lip or tongue swelling, dizziness or syncope, widespread swelling, mucosal, and known-trigger warnings while explicitly rejecting skin-only anaphylaxis inference.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: Recognises rapid symptoms with airway, breathing, or circulation problems and usually skin or mucosal changes such as urticaria or angioedema; skin changes alone are not anaphylaxis.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: Supports mapped food or medicine trigger, action-plan, return, and clinician-arranged allergy follow-up documentation after suspected anaphylaxis.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: Supports documenting known allergen exposure, food or drug triggers, action-plan discussion, and specialist follow-up after suspected anaphylaxis.

unresolved_source_gaps: Itching, episodic duration, dermographism, fever, broad allergy labels, blood tests, generic medication plans, fixed one-to-two-week follow-up, and routine urticaria management remain unsupported by opened exact sections.
Skin or mucosal changes alone must not be labelled anaphylaxis; acute urticaria remains clinician-stated.
Qualified clinician review has not been completed.
